Must I, or need I, explain that I am not against

the people who can’t bear “ mixed ”’ committees are, I

assume, willing to swallow the ““ Caius’’ gibe, and let

in Shakespeare. Now, T disapprove the Caius gibe, and

a good few other things in Shakespeare, but not being a

fool, or a moral weakling, I can benefit by the good in

the great man, and pass by the objectionable things.

supporting_documents.chunk_text:
capacity as politicians. Mr. Gwynn sees clearly the

absurdity of the theories of Mr. Edward Martyn's

party; he cannot be blind to the barrenness in practical

work of his own concerning many dopartments of

nation-making work.

In the course of a curious case tried at Olones

Quarter Sessions, Judge Craig said:—" They (the

Bungs) won’t make any distinction at all if they can

get money. These public-houses are carried on under

the most wicked and ruthless system. If they can get

money that is all they care for. They don’t care if it

comes from hell if they can rake it into their tills,

and they care nothing whatever if they are the means

of sending to hell, body and soul, the people from

whom they get it. But there is no use in my talking

supporting_documents.title: THE LEADER, VOL. XIV NO. 23, JULY 27 1907
supporting_documents.chunk_text:
political holiday for Englishmen and England’s Faith-

ful Garrison in Ireland by giving a display of barging

and bickering. The real work to be done in the living

present, despite our political disabilities, is being partly

overlooked, and it is being partly done by people who

are sick alike of the Parliamentary Party and of the

antics of Edward Martyn and Co.

It is easier to come to the front in what stands for

the political world in Ireland—for there is not much

skilled competition in the business—than it is to excel

as a manufacturer, a professional man, or an up-to-date

business man. An Empee or an anti-Empee does

not require as much skill as a creamery manager, a

T

skilled farmer, or a factory foreman, so there are

plenty of spouting politicians in the market—a market

on hhf) p}anhmg of trees and flowers for the shading and

beautifying of the approaches to the town would not

5 N be amiss. - At present the sun or rain or storm beat on

N one mercilessly. There is scarcely any green to rest the

eyes except the grass in the neighbouring fields, and a

few stunted hawthorns. Indeed, there is nothing,

save lack of ‘enterprise, to prevent a miniature Bois

being planted in a suburb of Ennis with shady

walks and drives for the delectation of the townsfolk.

During the winter months a series of ceilive, rgopal-

veacey, lectures, plays, and entertainments of various

kinds might be got up at the Town Hall. Any County

Council can obtain the services of trained lecturers from

the Department of Agriculture for the asking, who

‘to a happy fruition. Would we could all have done as

well. With Mr. Joyce also I remember attending an

Trish class at University College in the early days of

O’Growneyism, but though his sentiments are perhaps

not so different from those of the love-songs of Connacht,

T fear there is little trace of Gaelic inspiration in him,

And he drinks rather at the fountain of French verse.

Mr. M‘Donagh, too, strong Gael as he is, is mainly

-classical in' his literary affinities. ;

The question of the purpose of poetry is raised very

-strongly by these two books. Wherein shall it seek its

Tovelinéss? Beauty and joy is the theme of both writers,

and ecstacy their ‘mood; but ecstacy unopaline, not

hushful. Of the ““ Abbey ”” mood, the Yeatsian strain,

contradictory_documents.chunk_text:
act, they feared God and loved His Church. Ideal companions

they were, friends whose love was “ passing the love of woman.”

If T were engaged in a struggle for life, surrounded by

the most fearful dangers, I could not ask for more stalwart

hands or truer hearts than theirs to rush to my aid in the

Bearna Baoghail ; and if it were mine to choose the hands

that at the last would lower my body into its house of clay, I

would choose the strong, honest, tender hands of the Gaels

of Ireland! Grasa Dhe chughainn, agus bas ¢ n-Eirinn!

ONTARIO, CANADA,

January, 1907.

means his giving ‘‘up the demand for Irish nation-
“hood,”” is quite ab liberty to give a peaceful reception to
) - the British taxpayer and pay up his Income Tax with-
out prejudice to ‘‘ the demand for Irish nationhood ’’!
But Mr. Sweetman can withhold ‘“ what you call fair
- play to Mr. Birrell ” without risk to himself, but if he
withheld his Income Tax, well, then, his furniture would
be seized !

Mr. Sweetman proceeds:—‘‘In the latest published
letter of Mr. John Redmond he writes—* Of course, all
we ask is autonomy in purely Irish affairs.” This seems
to me to give up the right of nationhood for the Irish
nation. No Irishman, not even Mr. John Redmond,
has a right to do this. It is inherent in the nation and
cannot be resigned to a foreign power.”” No Irishman,

contextual_chunks.title: The Leader, vol. XV no. 19, December 28 1907
contextual_chunks.text:
30

The country had better keep a sharp eye on the
 ZIndependent. It has already, with the help of the
- Irish Times and other designing factors, inflicted Hum-
‘phrey’s Dump (Failed) on this country, and the unfor-
tunate guarantors have a simply deplorable prospect to
face. The Zudependent would like the people to forget
that it forced this calamity on Ireland. In a recent
report it suppressed the reference of Mr. Orr, a director
of Messrs. Thwaites & Co., to the Exhibition. It was at
its suppressing game again in connection with the sum-
mons re the Nigger minstrels at the late Exhibition.
Mr. William Gibson, who appeared for the coloured
lady, in the course of his statement as reported in the
Irish- Temes, said that “ Mrs. Roberts was the pro-
prietress of a negro threatrical troupe of ten or more
artistes, and that her agent was a Mr. Gannon, of
Bristol. In October last she was engaged by TFisher
and a Mr. Travers through Gannon to come over here
and perform at the International Exhibition. The Con-
cert Department of the Exhibition was under the direc-
tion of Mr. McGuckin, amongst whose paid licutenants
was Travers, who, it appeared, had associated him
